flashcards.q: What was the Old Regime?
flashcards.a: The social and political system in France before 1789, characterized by absolute monarchy and a society divided into three estates with privileges based on birth.

flashcards.q: Name the three estates of French society.
flashcards.a: First Estate (clergy), Second Estate (nobility), and Third Estate (everyone else including peasants, artisans, merchants, and professionals).

flashcards.q: What percentage of France's population belonged to the Third Estate?
flashcards.a: About 90% of the population, including peasants, artisans, landless labourers, servants, merchants, and professionals.

flashcards.q: What were the privileges enjoyed by the clergy and nobility?
flashcards.a: Exemption from paying taxes to the state, feudal dues from peasants (nobility), and tithes (clergy).

flashcards.q: What was the livre?
flashcards.a: The unit of currency in France before 1794.

flashcards.q: What was the taille?
flashcards.a: A direct tax paid to the state, imposed only on members of the Third Estate.

flashcards.q: What was the tithe?
flashcards.a: A tax levied by the church, comprising one-tenth of agricultural produce.

flashcards.q: What caused the subsistence crisis in France during the 1780s?
flashcards.a: Population growth, rising bread prices, stagnant wages, and bad harvests leading to food scarcity.

flashcards.q: Who were the key Enlightenment philosophers who influenced the revolution?
flashcards.a: John Locke, Jean Jacques Rousseau, and Montesquieu.

flashcards.q: What was Rousseau's major political idea?
flashcards.a: Government based on a social contract between people and their representatives, with popular sovereignty.

flashcards.q: What was Montesquieu's major contribution to political thought?
flashcards.a: Proposed division of power between legislative, executive, and judiciary branches.

flashcards.q: What was the Estates General?
flashcards.a: A political body to which the three estates sent representatives. The monarch could call its meeting to pass proposals for new taxes.

flashcards.q: When was the Estates General last convened before 1789?
flashcards.a: 1614, which was 175 years before the 1789 meeting.

flashcards.q: What was the Tennis Court Oath?
flashcards.a: On 20 June 1789, Third Estate representatives swore not to disperse until they had drafted a constitution for France.

flashcards.q: Who were Mirabeau and Abbé Sieyès?
flashcards.a: Leaders of the National Assembly. Mirabeau was a noble who supported the revolution; Sieyès wrote the influential pamphlet 'What is the Third Estate'.

flashcards.q: When did the storming of the Bastille take place?
What did the Bastille symbolize?
flashcards.a: The despotic power of the king and royal tyranny.

flashcards.q: What was the Great Fear?
flashcards.a: A period of panic and peasant revolts in the summer of 1789, triggered by rumours that nobles had hired brigands to destroy crops.

flashcards.q: What was abolished on 4 August 1789?
flashcards.a: The feudal system of obligations and taxes, along with the privileges of the clergy and nobility.

flashcards.q: What did the Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen proclaim?
flashcards.a: That men are born free and equal in rights; natural rights include liberty, property, security, and resistance to oppression; sovereignty resides in the nation.

flashcards.q: What rights did the Declaration of Rights guarantee?
flashcards.a: Right to life, freedom of speech, freedom of opinion, equality before law, and right to participate in law-making.

flashcards.q: Who were active citizens under the Constitution of 1791?
flashcards.a: Men above 25 years who paid taxes equal to at least 3 days of a labourer's wage. They had voting rights.

flashcards.q: Who were passive citizens?
flashcards.a: The remaining men, all women, children, and youth below 25 who had no voting rights.

flashcards.q: How many active citizens were there in France in 1791?
flashcards.a: About 4 million out of a total population of 28 million.

flashcards.q: What form of government did the Constitution of 1791 establish?
flashcards.a: A constitutional monarchy, with powers divided among legislature, executive, and judiciary.

flashcards.q: What was the Jacobin club?
flashcards.a: A political club whose members belonged mainly to less prosperous sections of society. It was named after the former convent of St Jacob in Paris.

flashcards.q: Who was the leader of the Jacobins?
flashcards.a: Maximilian Robespierre.

flashcards.q: What does 'sans-culottes' mean?
flashcards.a: Literally 'those without knee breeches', referring to Jacobins who wore long trousers to distinguish themselves from nobles who wore knee breeches.

flashcards.q: What did the red cap symbolize?
flashcards.a: Liberty. Sans-culottes men wore the red cap as a revolutionary symbol.

flashcards.q: When did France become a republic?
flashcards.a: 21 September 1792, when the Convention abolished the monarchy.

flashcards.q: When was Louis XVI executed?
flashcards.a: 21 January 1793, on the charge of treason.

flashcards.q: What was the Reign of Terror?
flashcards.a: The period from 1793 to 1794 when Robespierre and the Jacobins implemented severe policies, executing thousands considered enemies of the republic.

flashcards.q: What was the guillotine?
flashcards.a: A device consisting of two poles and a blade used for beheading, named after Dr Guillotin who invented it.

flashcards.q: What was the Directory?
flashcards.a: A five-member executive appointed by legislative councils under the Constitution of 1795. It ruled France from 1795 to 1799.

flashcards.q: Who was Olympe de Gouges?
flashcards.a: A revolutionary woman who wrote the Declaration of the Rights of Woman and Citizen in 1791, demanding equal political rights for women.

flashcards.q: When did French women finally win the right to vote?
What was the triangular slave trade?
flashcards.a: A three-part trade route: French merchants sailed to Africa to buy slaves, transported them to Caribbean colonies, and returned with colonial products like sugar and coffee.

flashcards.q: When was slavery first abolished in French colonies?
flashcards.a: 1794 by the Convention.

flashcards.q: Who reintroduced slavery in French colonies?
flashcards.a: Napoleon Bonaparte in 1804.

flashcards.q: When was slavery finally abolished in French colonies?
What was the Marseillaise?
flashcards.a: A patriotic song composed by Roget de L'Isle; it became the national anthem of France.

flashcards.q: What change regarding censorship occurred after the revolution?
flashcards.a: Censorship was abolished; freedom of speech and expression was proclaimed as a natural right.

flashcards.q: Who became Emperor of France in 1804?
flashcards.a: Napoleon Bonaparte.

flashcards.q: When and where was Napoleon finally defeated?
flashcards.a: Waterloo in 1815.

flashcards.q: What were the most important legacies of the French Revolution?
flashcards.a: The ideas of liberty, equality, democratic rights, and popular sovereignty, which spread across Europe and inspired anti-colonial movements worldwide.

flashcards.q: What is a republic?
flashcards.a: A form of government where the people elect the government, including the head of state, with no hereditary monarchy.

flashcards.q: What was the significance of 5 October 1789?
flashcards.a: Thousands of Parisian women marched to Versailles, demanding bread and bringing the king back to Paris.
